case_name: Professional Conduct Committee appointed by the Medical Council of New Zealand v R [2019] NZHPDT 1057 (18 December 2019)

summary.brief: Professional Conduct Committee appointed by the Medical Council of New Zealand v R [2019] NZHPDT 1057 (18 December 2019). Medical malpractice case involving oncology.

evidence_index.text: Dr R indiscriminately used pethidine for Mrs N for her various problems including headaches of various types and that the dosages were often excessive and frequent.
evidence_index.speaker: PCC Dr Harrison

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal was not satisfied on the evidence that this had been inappropriate or excessive. There was no evidence of development of dependence on the drug or any complications arising from its use.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal

evidence_index.text: Dr Harrison evidence - Mrs N described as '2 – 3 years', although he had been asked about the period 1 January 2014 to 1 January 2017, a period of three years.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr Harrison

evidence_index.text: Dr R’s conduct, as detailed above, was in breach of his ethical obligations and/or legal obligations and/or accepted standards of practice.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal

evidence_index.text: The purpose of the disciplinary procedure is the protection of the public by the maintenance of professional standards.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal

evidence_index.text: Dr R failed to accurately and/or adequately document the prescribing of pethidine in Ms N’s patient records.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal

evidence_index.text: The conduct alleged above amounts to professional misconduct in that, either separately or cumulatively, it: amounts to malpractice and/or negligence.
evidence_index.speaker: Tribunal

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient has a history of chronic daily headaches and migraines, treated with pethidine.

focused_exam.neurological: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

requestables.name: Pain Clinic Referral
requestables.description: Referral to Pain Clinic for migraine management

reveal.result_summary: Referral noted concerns about medication overuse and recommended alternative treatments.
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates need for change in management strategy.
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt review of current medication regimen.

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with chronic migraines managed with pethidine.

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with chronic migraines on pethidine
decision_points.prompt: What should be the approach to managing the patient's migraines?

options.clinical_reasoning: Patient reports relief with pethidine, but risks of dependency and overuse are high.
Standard practice to avoid opioids for migraine due to risk of medication overuse headaches.

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed it was the only effective treatment

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence and guidelines against opioid use for migraines

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Continued use of pethidine despite known risks and recommendations against it.
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Patient should have been transitioned to alternative migraine management.
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Breach of standard care for migraine management

key_findings: Breach of duty in continuing pethidine despite risks
Failure to transition to alternative migraine management

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Alternative management would have reduced medication overuse

factual_timeline.event: Patient treated with pethidine for migraines

treatments.outcome: Temporary relief but risk of dependency

court_findings.finding: Dr R breached duty of care
court_findings.legal_significance: Failure to adhere to standard migraine management practices
